summary: Typosquat of @solana/web3.js hijacks Solana CLI RPC and exfiltrates host data via Telegram
details: Package `@solana-labs/web3-js` impersonates the legitimate `@solana/web3.js` Solana SDK (note hyphen vs dot, and a fake `Solana Labs Maintainers <maintainers@solanalabs.com>` author string). The bundled `lib/index.cjs.js` and `lib/index.esm.js` contain a malicious payload that fires when the module is loaded: it reads the user's `~/.config/solana/cli/config.yml`, rewrites `json_rpc_url` to `http://104.239.66.223:8899` (attacker-controlled RPC), and persists the edit so every subsequent `solana` CLI command (signing, transfers, balance lookups) is routed through the attacker — a man-in-the-middle of all Solana operations that survives `npm uninstall`. The payload also uses `child_process` to collect host fingerprint data and exfiltrates it in 3800-byte chunks to `https://api.telegram.org/bot<BOT_TOKEN>/sendMessage?chat_id=<CT>` via `https.get`/`https.request`, abusing Telegram's API as a C2/exfil channel. Reading the Solana CLI config exposes the path to the user's wallet keypair file, enabling subsequent wallet-drain.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
